coredata.dc:title: B and N Co-doped carbon dots for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of riboflavin and information security with room temperature phosphorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Na
Yan, Yanan
Sun, Ning
Huang, Yue
Liu, Yang
Dong, Wenjuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
Zhao, Jie
Wang, Ruinbing
Song, Shengmei
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: Matrix-free room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have generated considerable interest in sensing, anti-counterfeiting and bioimaging due to long lifetime and large Stokes shift. However, spin-forbidden transition and non-radiative transition make RTP challenging to implement. In this work, matrix-free RTP carbon dots (CDs) co-doped with boron and nitrogen were prepared through the microwave-assisted strategy. The CDs realize RTP without any protective matrix. This may be attributed to the stable and rigid structure formed during the heating polymerization process, which can protect the triplet excited state from being quenched by oxygen and water in the air environment. The fluorescence and phosphorescence emission wavelength of the as-prepared CDs were observed at 532 nm and 596 nm with the excitation wavelength of 476 nm and 514 nm, respectively, showing a long afterglow lifetime of 735.3 ms. Nitrogen doping has a crucial effect on the luminescence of RTP, and the increase of nitrogen content facilitates the n-π* transition and improve the efficiency of intersystem crossing (ISC). Importantly, the ratiometric fluorescence method for determining riboflavin has been established based on the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the CDs (Donor) and riboflavin (Acceptor), with a linear relationship in the range of 9.52–85.71 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) of 19.9 nM. Additionally, the phosphorescent property of the CDs was exploited to demonstrate the potential applications in the field of information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Riboflavin
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of riboflavin by the CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM, HRTEM (inset) images and (B) the size distribution of CDs.
(A) FT-IR spectrum; (B) XPS full-survey and high-resolution of (C) C 1s, (D) N 1s, (E) O 1s and (F) B 1s for the CDs.

UV–vis absorption (A) and fluorescence luminescence (FL) spectra (B) of the CDs in aqueous solution. (C) Fluorescence and phosphorescence luminescence (PL) spectra of the CDs powder. (D) The phosphorescent attenuation spectrum of CDs, illustration is the luminescence of the CDs in the on/off state of 365 nm ultraviolet lamps.

(A) Fluorescence of CDs in the presence of riboflavin (0–133.33 μM), the insert is relationship between I524 nm/I384 nm and riboflavin concentration in the range from 0 to 133.33 μM. (B) The linear relationship between I524 nm/I384 nm and riboflavin concentration in the range from 9.52–85.71 μM. (C–D) The anti-interference experiments of coexisting substances (Pro, Phe, Gln, Lys, Met, Asn, Glu, VB3, VB1, PG, Thi, DH, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, I−, Br−, CO3 2−, P2O7 4−, Cd2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Pb2+) on the determination of riboflavin by the prepared CDs.
(A) The black shaded area, the absorption of riboﬂavin (green line) and the CDs emission spectra (red line). (B) Attenuation spectra of CDs reacting with riboflavin of different concentrations.
(A)The photograph of CDs powder under UV lamping and UV lamping off. (B) Information encryption application of CDs.
Determination of riboflavin in real samples.

head.ce:title: B and N Co-doped carbon dots for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of riboflavin and information security with room temperature phosphorescence

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Taipa, 999078, Macau

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Matrix-free room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have generated considerable interest in sensing, anti-counterfeiting and bioimaging due to long lifetime and large Stokes shift. However, spin-forbidden transition and non-radiative transition make RTP challenging to implement. In this work, matrix-free RTP carbon dots (CDs) co-doped with boron and nitrogen were prepared through the microwave-assisted strategy. The CDs realize RTP without any protective matrix. This may be attributed to the stable and rigid structure formed during the heating polymerization process, which can protect the triplet excited state from being quenched by oxygen and water in the air environment. The fluorescence and phosphorescence emission wavelength of the as-prepared CDs were observed at 532 nm and 596 nm with the excitation wavelength of 476 nm and 514 nm, respectively, showing a long afterglow lifetime of 735.3 ms. Nitrogen doping has a crucial effect on the luminescence of RTP, and the increase of nitrogen content facilitates the n-π* transition and improve the efficiency of intersystem crossing (ISC). Importantly, the ratiometric fluorescence method for determining riboflavin has been established based on the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the CDs (Donor) and riboflavin (Acceptor), with a linear relationship in the range of 9.52–85.71 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) of 19.9 nM. Additionally, the phosphorescent property of the CDs was exploited to demonstrate the potential applications in the field of information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials exhibits superior performance in sensing, bioimaging, photoelectricity and safety encryption [1]. Heretofore, inorganic complexes doped with rare earth metals and organometallic compounds have been extensively studied, which can realize RTP by designing and decoration. However, these materials demand rigorous synthetic conditions and expensive raw materials, which substantially limits the application and development of RTP materials. Consequently, it is necessary to develop RTP materials with good luminescence performance, non-toxicity, facile preparation and environmental friendliness.

As a new zero-dimensional nanometer material, carbon dots (CDs) own several advantages, such as stable photoluminescence, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and environmental friendliness. Therefore, CDs have been widely applied in the fields of sensing analysis [2], biological imaging [3], optoelectronics [4] and catalysis [5]. Notably, CDs have also been reported to exhibit room-temperature phosphorescence. It is worth noting that RTP CDs were synthesized by embedding them in matrices, such as polyvinyl alcohol [6], polyacrylamide [7], urea [8], boric acid [9], zeolites [10] and gelatin [11], to suppress the vibration dissipation between triplet and singlet states. Nevertheless, picking a appropriate substrate is ambitious, and the preparation procedure is often sophisticated. In recent years, matrix-free RTP CDs have also been reported. Nie [12] et al. prepared nitrogen-doped CDs (n-CDs) with RTP using urea, tartaric acid and acrylamide as raw materials by a one-step hydrothermal method. The n-CDs can also be used in the field of advanced information security as an encryption ink. In order to enable phosphorescence to be captured by the naked eyes easily, some RTP CDs have been synthesized with long lifetime. He [13] et al. obtained the matrix-free RTP CDs with efficient phosphorescence properties. The CDs have a long RTP lifetime of 1.22 s and a high quantum yield of 21.6 %, which provide a new perspective for the perspective matrix-free CDs. In 2020, Feng [14]et al. developed a convenient, rapid and gram-scale method to obtain boron and halogen doped CDs and exhibit ultralong lifetime of 2.61 s due to external heavy-atom effect. In summary, construction of matrix-free RTP CDs with long lifetime is a research hotspot.

Riboflavin (VB2) is slightly soluble in water and has been linked to human health and can participate in the metabolism of sugar, protein and fat, and promote cell growth and development [15]. Lack of riboflavin can cause metabolic disorders, resulting in diseases such as keratitis, tongue inflammation, conjunctivitis and seborrheic dermatitis [16]. Riboflavin cannot be synthesized in the human body and needs to be supplemented by food or nutrition. Accordingly, it is of great significance to monitor the riboflavin in drugs and food [17]. The existing techniques for the detection of riboflavin include electrochemical analysis [18], high performance liquid chromatography [19], molecular imprinting [20] and capillary electrophoresis [21], which mainly show some kinds of drawbacks such as the cumbersome sample preparation, expensive instruments, or complicated operating procedure, certainly limiting their potential applications. In contrast, fluorescence spectroscopy has attracted much attention because of its simplicity, low cost and high sensitivity. Consequently, developing the accurate strategies of detecting VB2 is still meaningful.

Herein, nitrogen and boron co-doped phosphorescent CDs were synthesized by using resorcinol, boric acid (BA) and 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) through the microwave-assisted strategy, which exhibited blue fluorescence in aqueous solution and green phosphorescence in the powder under UV lamp. The existence of the doped N is related to the RTP of the CDs because it promotes the n-π* transition and facilitates intersystem crossing (ISC), which allowed the excited triplet state was filled. Moreover, the ratiometric fluorescence method for riboflavin was established based on the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the CDs (Donor) and riboflavin (Acceptor) with the linear relationship in the range of 9.52–85.71 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) of 19.9 nM. Notably, the developed method was simple, economically and environmentally friendly, and the CDs process the RTP property. Based on the feature, the CDs showed the potentiality of information encryption.

Boric acid was offered from Tianjin Hengxing chemical reagent manufacturing Co., Ltd. 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA) and amino acids were bought from Sigma (Shanghai, China). Others were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China).
All characterizations of the as-synthesized CDs referring our previously published articles [22–24].

The preparation method of CDs is described as follow (Fig. 1 ). Resorcinol (1.10 g, 0.01 mol) and boric acid (1.85 g, 0.03 mol) were loaded into a beaker. And 100 μL EDA was dissolved in 10 mL ultra-pure water and transferred into the beaker. The mixed solution was obtained by ultrasound for 3 min, then heated at 400 W power for 14 min in a household microwave oven. After the reaction mixture being cooled to room temperature naturally, a certain amount of ultrapure water was added into the mixture and the solid was dissolved by sonicate completely. The CDs solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm filter membrane and then dialysis treatment was carried out for 12 h (1000 Da). The water was changed every 4 h. The CDs aqueous solution was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for the future application.

Synthesis of CDs-1. CDs-1 was prepared by the same method as above using resorcinol and boric acid dissolved in 10 mL of water and sonicated to mix thoroughly. The remaining steps are the same as those for synthesizing the CDs. The difference is that CDs-1 does not exhibit phosphorescent properties because EDA was not added during the preparation of CDs-1.

100 μL CDs solution (40.1 mg/mL) was dissolved in 2 mL of water followed by different concentrations of riboflavin solution, and fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 304 nm. In order to evaluate the selectivity of CDs, 30 μL of 13 metal ions (K+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Na+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ag+, Mg2+), 16 anions (C2O4 2−, S2−, S2O3 2−, H2PO4 −, Ac−, NO3 −, SO3 2−, F−, SCN−, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, I−, Br−, CO3 2−, P2O7 4−), 18 amino acids (Asparticacid/Asp, lsoleucine/Ile, Glycine/Gly, Valine/Val, Leucine/Leu, Threonine/Thr, Alanine/Ala, Cysteine/Cys, Glutathione/GSH, Serine/Ser, Arginine/Arg, Proline/Pro, Phenylalanine/Phe, Glutamine/Gln, Lysine/Lys, Methionine/Met, Asparagine/Asn, Glutamicacid/Glu) and 14 antibiotics (Doxorubicin/Dox, Amoxicillin/AML, Clarithromycin/CLARY, Tetracycline/TC, Velosef, Kanamycin/Kana, Azithromycin/Azi, Gentamycin/GM, Chlorotetracycline/CTC, Penicillin/PG, Tigecycline/THi, Tinidazole/TNZ, Ornidazole/ONZ, Dopamine hydrochloride/DH) and 5 vitamins (Vitamin B6/VB6, Biotin, Vitamin B3/VB3, Vitamin B1/VB1, Riboflavin/VB2) with a concentration of 0.01 M were removed and added into CDs solution, and the fluorescence intensities were recorded after full mixing. To evaluate the interference of coexisting substances on the determination of riboflavin, the fluorescence intensities of CDs solution were measured after CDs solution being mixed with some co-existing substances (0.01 M, 100 μL), and then riboflavin was added into the mixed solution and the fluorescence intensities were recorded.

Two riboflavin pharmaceutical tables were purchased from a local supermarket (Taiyuan, China). One tablet of Wuchuan VB2（riboflavin tablet 1）and one table of Guanyaoshi VB2（riboflavin tablet 2）were ground into powder and dissolved into 5 mL of ethanol solution, respectively. They were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to drop the insoluble materials and obtain the supernatant. Then the large particles were removed through a 0.22 μm membrane filter. Finally, 5 mL of a clarified riboflavin solution were collected and stored at 4 °C.

The dimension and morphology of the CDs were determined by TEM (Fig. 2 A). The as-prepared CDs were almost uniform and monodisperse with an average diameter of 3.91 nm (Fig. 2B). The results from High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 2A) depicted that the interlayer lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which matched with the (100) diffraction planes of graphitic structure [25].

The chemical structures and surface functional groups of the CDs were analyzed using elemental analysis, XPS and FT-IR. Through Table S1, the CDs are composed of C, H, O, N and B, and the contents are 26.37 %, 4.94 %, 28.82 %, 1.43 % and 38.44 % (calculated), respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 A, FT-IR spectroscopy of the CDs display an absorption peak at 3213 cm−1, which indicates the existence of many N–H and O–H groups [26]. The characteristic peaks located at 1611 cm−1, 1399 cm−1, 1311 cm−1 and 1214 cm−1, respectively, are derived from the stretching vibration of CC/N, C–O–C, C–N and C–O [27–29]. Three distinct absorption peaks with boron-containing groups at 1495 cm−1, 1147 cm−1 and 961 cm−1 correspond to B–O, B–O–H and B–C stretching vibrations, respectively [30]. The new band at 767 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of unsaturated C–H. These results demonstrate that B and N have been doped into the CDs successfully. Furthermore, the high-resolution peaks of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and B 1s were identified at 280.5 eV, 527.4 eV, 397.1 eV and 187.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 3B). The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) demonstrates three peaks at 284.8, 286.4, and 290.2 eV, which belongs to C–C, C–N/C–O, and COOH, respectively [31]. The N 1s bands (Fig. 3D) can be deconstructed into three peaks at 399.8 eV, 401.6 eV, and 402.2 eV, which are mainly in the form of pyrrolic N, N–H and graphitic N, respectively [32]. The O 1s peak (Fig. 3E) exhibits two peaks at 532.5 eV and 533.4 eV for C–O and CO, respectively [33]. Three peaks at 193.0 eV, 195.2 eV and 198.9 eV in the B 1s spectrum (Fig. 3F) are corresponding to BCO2, B2O3 and B–O, respectively [34]. XPS analysis is fully consistent with FT-IR result. The results of FT-IR and XPS analysis shown that the surface of the CDs contained abundant functional groups of CN/C–N, favoring RTP emission [35].

Subsequently, the optical properties of the CDs were examined by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra in detail. The CDs display a distinct peak at 273 nm (Fig. 4 A), which belong to n-π* transition of the CN or CO. The optimal excitation and emission peaks of the CDs in water solution are located at 304 nm and 384 nm, respectively. And the emission wavelength of the CDs is dependent of the excitation wavelength (Fig. S1). The excitation-dependent property suggested the presence of multiple emission channels for the as-prepared CDs, which is a common phenomenon for numerous other CDs. The relative fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs at 304 nm was calculated to be 9.12 % in aqueous solution under the optimal excitation (Quinine sulfate as a reference) (Fig. S2). The CDs in aqueous solution exhibits blue fluorescence but no phosphorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Notably, Fig. 4C exhibits the RTP phenomenon of the CDs powder and the RTP could be recognized by the naked eye (Illustration of Fig. 4D). The phosphorescence emission maxima of the CDs were observed at 596 nm (purple line) under the optimal excitation wavelength of 514 nm (blue line). Simultaneously, the fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths of the CDs powder are 476 and 532 nm, respectively. According to the attenuation spectrum and fitting data in Fig. 4D and Table S3, the mean lifetime of the CDs powder is calculated to be 735.34 ms.

As shown in Fig. S3, after continuous irradiation with Xenon lamp (120 min) and UV lamp (120 min), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs changed slightly, which demonstrates that the CDs has high anti-photobleaching performance. Meanwhile, the high ionic strength (2.0 M) KCl and wide pH range (3.0–10.0) have no significant quenching effect for fluorescence intensity of the CDs. In addition, the CDs powder was tested by irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp, the phosphorescence intensity remained stable, indicating that the CDs have excellent resistance to photobleaching. Above results justify that the CDs possess excellent fluorescence and phosphorescence stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of riboflavin by the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the abundance of functional groups on the surface of CDs, their application in analysis was investigated. Common metal cations, anions, amino acids, antibiotics and vitamin were selected for determination. Fig. S4 represents the fluorescence response of the CDs to different substances. Only the addition of riboflavin enhanced its fluorescence intensity at 524 nm, while the addition of other substances barely changed the fluorescence intensity of CDs, indicating that the CDs had a good specific response to riboflavin. The fluorescence intensity at 384 nm of the CDs gradual decrease with a novel emission peak at 524 nm when the concentration of riboflavin increases (Fig. 5 A). And there was an excellent linear relationship between the FL efficiency (I 524 nm/I 384 nm) and the riboflavin concentration in the range of 9.52–85.71 μM. Relationship between the two was I 524 nm/I 384 nm = 0.02582Criboflavin – 0.08004 (n = 25) with the correlation coefficient of 0.996 and the limit of detection (LOD) of 19.9 nM. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CDs did not distinct change to various interfering ions and related antibiotics (Fig. 5C and D), which verified that the CDs have good selectivity and anti-interference properties for riboflavin detection.

UV–vis absorption spectra and lifetime measurement demonstrated the reaction mechanism. No new peak appeared in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs when riboflavin of different concentrations was added (Fig. S5), so static quenching effect could be excluded. In Fig. 6 A, there is a large overlap between the emission peak of CDs and the UV–vis absorption of riboflavin, indicating that the FRET system was conditional to be established between the CDs and riboflavin. And the distance and transfer efficiency between the donor (CDs) and the acceptor (riboflavin) can be verified by calculating. Based the donor-recipient energy transfer theory, the distance R0 between CDs and riboflavin can be calculated by the following formula [36]:
R 0 6
=
9000
[
ln
10
]
κ 2
φ D
128
π 5
N
n 4
J
( λ )

κ2 is usually assumed to be 2/3. And φD is the quantum yield of the donor, which is about 9.12 %. n is the refractive index of the medium, which is 1.4. J(λ) is the index of the spectral overlap integral between the donor and the acceptor, which can be calculated as 0.94 × 10−14 cm3 M−1. The R0 calculated to be 1.07 nm, which conforms that the distance satisfies the requirements to construct the FRET system (1 nm < R0 < 10 nm). According to the transfer efficiency η of FRET [37] (η = 1-I/I
0 , where I and I
0 represent the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 384 nm in the presence and absence of riboflavin, respectively), the η is increased from 0 % to 57.0 % with the addition of riboflavin from 0 μM to 61.9 μM. The average lifetime of CDs changed from 6.91 ns to 5.85 ns when lower concentration of riboflavin was added, which is attributed to dynamic quenching. When the concentrations of riboflavin were gradually increased, the average life remained essentially unchanged (Fig. 6B and Table S4). Therefore, the interaction between CDs and riboflavin is caused by a combination of dynamic quenching and internal filtration effect.

Testing the accuracy of methods by detecting different pharmaceutical tablets. The results of the sample analysis were given in Table 1 , the recoveries in real samples varied from 97.89 to 102.95 % with relative standard deviations (RSD) from 1.65 to 3.05 %, which demonstrate the applicability of the selected method.

To sum up, the formation of the optical properties of CDs can be tentatively proposed in the following. According to previous reports, –NH2 group is the main luminescent center of phosphorescent emission in RTP materials [38–40]. At the same time, ethylenediamine has no branched chain structure, resulting in a small steric hindrance, which enable the cross-linking reaction and form a strong crosslinked skeleton [35]. The CO and CN-related subfluorophores with strong spin-orbit coupling facilitate the generation of excited triplets more efficiently through ISC [41]. And the result of FT-IR and XPS demonstrate that abundant functional groups (CO and CN) of CDs contribute to afterglow emission. In this work, CDs-1 without nitrogen doping was synthesized, and almost no phosphorescence was observed (Fig. S6). In another group of comparison experiments, CDs-50, CDs-80, CDs-100, CDs-120 and CDs-150 with different nitrogen content were synthesized by the same method (representing ethylenediamine content of 50 μL, 80 μL, 100 μL, 120 μL and 150 μL, respectively). It can be seen that the luminous efficiency of phosphorescence gradually increases before the amount of N addition reaches 120, indicating that the doping of N has a crucial influence on the luminous effect of RTP. Nitrogen has an electronic structure similar to that of carbon atoms, which improves phosphorescence quantum yield. The nitrogen-containing group facilitates the n-π* transition, which increases the efficiency of inter-system crossing and protects the triplet exciton from bursts of oxygen and water in the air environment.

Information protection is of great significance in the information era, and the long lifetime is a necessary condition for RTP materials to be applied in anti-counterfeiting. As shown in Fig. 7 A, CDs powder exhibits a long phosphorescence duration. The as-prepared CDs and CDs-1 (without phosphorescence) were successfully applied to information encryption (Fig. 7B). The information “SXU” and “8888” can be observed under the 365 nm UV lamp. When the excitation light source was turned off, the real information “SX” and “2022” can be observed due to the difference in the lifetime of fluorescence and phosphorescence. Due to the simplicity of the CDs preparation method, it indicates that the CDs could be widely used in a wide range of applications such as anti-counterfeit labels.

In summary, the matrix-free, efficient and environmentally friendly phosphorescent CDs were prepared by using resorcinol, boric acid and ethylenediamine. The characterization analysis revealed that the synthesized CDs surface contains abundant CN/C–N functional groups, which provides favorable conditions for the realization of RTP. In addition, comparative experiments illustrated that the increase of nitrogen content effectively improved the luminous efficiency of RTP. At the same time, the prepared CDs can be used for the ratio detection of riboflavin. The raw materials of the CDs are easy to being obtained, and the prepared method is low cost and efficient. Moreover, the prepared CDs have potential application in analysis, detection and information security.

Na Wei: Writing – original draft. Yanan Yan: Writing – review & editing. Ning Sun: Conceptualization. Yue Huang: Data curation. Yang Liu: Investigation. Wenjuan Dong: Software. Xiaojuan Gong: Writing – review & editing. Jie Zhao: Supervision. Ruinbing Wang: Writing – review & editing. Shengmei Song: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chuan Dong: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in the paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22274090), Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Classical Prescription Strengthening Yang (No. 202104010910011), the Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (No. 202203021221026) and the Shanxi Province Patent Conversion Project (No. 202306012).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor-emissive carbon dots for white-light-emitting diodes and room-temperature phosphorescence
ACS Appl. Nano Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exploration of room-temperature phosphorescence and new mechanism on carbon dots in a polyacrylamide platform and their applications for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption
Adv. Opt. Mater.

Chen, Exploration of room-temperature phosphorescence and new mechanism on carbon dots in a polyacrylamide platform and their applications for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption, Adv Opt Mater, 2022, 10(15) :2200678.
